name: Cox's Bazar
shortDesc: World's longest natural sea beach — 120km of golden sands along the Bay of Bengal.
overview: Cox's Bazar is the crown jewel of Bangladesh's tourism, boasting a 120-kilometer unbroken coastline along the Bay of Bengal. Beyond just lounging by the sea, the area offers a vibrant coastal culture, bustling seafood markets, and an energetic atmosphere. Marine Drive offers a scenic drive with ocean on one side and green hills on the other. Himchari provides panoramic sea views while Inani Beach features unique coral boulders. Water sports including parasailing, jet-skiing, and beach biking are available at Sugandha and Laboni points.

name: Sundarbans
shortDesc: World's largest mangrove forest — UNESCO World Heritage home of the Royal Bengal Tiger.
overview: This colossal delta region, formed by the confluence of the Ganges, Brahmaputra, and Meghna rivers, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The Sundarbans is an intricate network of tidal waterways, mudflats, and small islands of salt-tolerant mangrove forests. Wildlife spotting includes the elusive Royal Bengal Tiger, spotted deer, wild boars, and saltwater crocodiles. Eco-tourism trails at Karamjal and Katka offer wooden walkways through pristine mangrove habitats. Birding is exceptional — hundreds of species including kingfishers, eagles, and herons.

name: Sajek Valley
shortDesc: Queen of Hills — clouds drift below your feet at 1,800 feet above sea level.
overview: Geographically located in the Rangamati district but accessible via Khagrachari, Sajek Valley sits at an altitude of about 1,800 feet. It is famous for its dramatic weather changes — you can experience bright sunshine, sudden rain, and heavy fog all in a single day. Konglak Para offers a spectacular 360-degree view of the surrounding Lusai Hills stretching into India. Wake up early to watch a literal sea of clouds rolling beneath the mountain peaks. Indigenous Tripura and Lushai communities offer unique cultural experiences.

name: Saint Martin's Island
shortDesc: Bangladesh's only coral reef island — crystal water, sea turtles, and coral reefs.
overview: Locally known as Narikel Jinjira (Coconut Island), this tiny landmass in the northeastern part of the Bay of Bengal is Bangladesh's only coral reef island. Its isolation keeps the water incredibly clear and the skies free from urban light pollution. Chhera Dwip is an adjacent uninhabited coral island reachable by wooden boat or walk during low tide. Snorkeling and scuba reveal vibrant underwater marine life. The entire island perimeter can be cycled in just a couple of hours.

name: Srimangal
shortDesc: Tea capital of Bangladesh — 7-layer tea, Lawachara rainforest, and hoolock gibbons.
overview: Nestled in the Moulvibazar district, Srimangal is characterized by endless miles of rolling hills carpeted with emerald-green tea estates. The region receives high rainfall, making its flora incredibly lush and dense. Lawachara National Park is a tropical semi-evergreen forest where the rare Hoolock Gibbons can be spotted swinging through the canopy. The famous seven-layer tea at Nilkantha Tea Cabin is a must-try — each layer has a distinct color and flavor. Madhabpur Lake is surrounded by hills and blooming lotus flowers.

name: Kuakata
shortDesc: The only beach in Bangladesh where you can see both sunrise AND sunset over the sea.
overview: Located at the southernmost tip of Bangladesh, Kuakata is a relatively quiet beach town offering a rare geographical advantage: an unobstructed view of both the sun rising from the sea and setting into the sea. This rare dual phenomenon earns it the name 'Sagar Kanya' (Daughter of the Sea). Fatrar Char is a mangrove forest extension of the Sundarbans ecosystem. The area has cultural landmarks including a 100-year-old Buddhist statue at Misripara and the ancient Kuakata well.

name: Bandarban
shortDesc: Adventure capital of Bangladesh — highest peaks, roaring waterfalls, and tribal culture.
overview: Bandarban is the ultimate destination for adventure seekers in Bangladesh. It houses the highest mountain peaks in the country and is crisscrossed by the winding, majestic Sangu River. Trekking routes lead to formidable peaks like Keokradong, or brave trails to the roaring Nafakhum and Amiakhum waterfalls. Nilgiri and Nilachal hill stations offer breathtaking cloud-level views of the sprawling hill tracts. The Buddha Dhatu Jadi Golden Temple is an intricately designed Theravada Buddhist temple perched on a hilltop.

name: Rangamati
shortDesc: Lake city with the hanging bridge — Kaptai Lake glittering through green hilltops.
overview: Rangamati's landscape is dominated by Kaptai Lake, a massive artificial lake created in the 1960s. The town is a beautiful blend of water, green hills, and vibrant indigenous culture, particularly that of the Chakma people. Kaptai Lake cruises on traditional wooden boats wind through gorges and hillocks. Shuvolong Waterfall is exceptionally magnificent during the monsoon season, accessible by boat. The iconic Jhulonto (Hanging) Bridge suspension bridge has become the prime symbol of Rangamati.

name: Paharpur Buddhist Monastery
shortDesc: 8th century UNESCO heritage — largest Buddhist monastery south of the Himalayas.
overview: Historically known as Somapura Mahavihara, this 8th-century archaeological site in Naogaon district is a testament to the Pala Dynasty's architectural brilliance. It was once one of the most prominent centers of Buddhist learning in South Asia. The hundreds of intricate terracotta tiles detail daily life, flora, and fauna of ancient Bengal. The massive, cruciform-shaped central stupa dominates the complex. The site museum displays recovered artifacts, statues of Hindu and Buddhist deities, and ancient coins.

name: Lalbagh Fort
shortDesc: 17th century Mughal fort in Old Dhaka — prince's palace, secret tunnels, and marble mausoleums.
overview: An incomplete 17th-century Mughal fort complex sitting by the Buriganga River in Old Dhaka. Initiated by Prince Muhammad Azam, son of Emperor Aurangzeb, it remains one of the finest examples of Mughal architecture in the country. The Tomb of Pari Bibi is uniquely constructed using black basalt, white marble, and encaustic tiles. Diwan-i-Aam houses a museum of Mughal weaponry, calligraphy, and coins. The grounds contain blocked entrances to rumored secret labyrinthine escape tunnels.

name: Ahsan Manzil
shortDesc: The Pink Palace of Dhaka — Nawab's 19th century riverside Indo-Saracenic masterpiece.
overview: Known for its striking pink facade, Ahsan Manzil was the official residential palace and seat of the Nawab of Dhaka. Built in the mid-19th century, this grand structure reflects Indo-Saracenic Revival architecture. Walk up the sweeping majestic staircase leading from the riverfront to the main palace. The 23 restored rooms display period furniture, crystal chandeliers, and the Nawabs' personal belongings. The striking pink color against the backdrop of the Buriganga River makes it exceptional for architectural photography.

name: Ratargul Swamp Forest
shortDesc: Amazon of Bangladesh — a freshwater swamp forest where boats glide under submerged trees.
overview: Often referred to as the 'Amazon of Bangladesh,' Ratargul is a freshwater swamp forest in Sylhet. During the monsoon, the water level rises dramatically, submerging the lower trunks of the dense Koroch trees. Hire a local wooden boat to glide silently through the flooded, narrow channels beneath a canopy of green. The forest watchtower offers a sweeping bird's-eye view of the entire swamp and the distant Meghalaya hills. Wildlife includes monkeys, snakes, and a wide variety of local and migratory birds.

name: Bichanakandi
shortDesc: Crystal streams from Meghalaya hills flow over coloured boulders at the India-Bangladesh border.
overview: Located at the Bangladesh-India border in Sylhet, Bichanakandi is where several mountain streams flowing from the Meghalaya hills converge. The area acts as a natural stone quarry, with cold, crystal-clear water flowing over colorful boulders. Wade and swim in the freezing, clear water streaming down from the Indian hills. The dramatic contrast of massive, dark green mountains towering directly over the rocky riverbed creates stunning landscape photography opportunities. The journey itself involves a highly scenic boat ride down the Piyain River.

name: Jaflong
shortDesc: Stone collections, Dawki River, and the foot of the Khasi-Jaintia range at the India border.
overview: Situated at the foothills of the Khasi-Jaintia range, Jaflong is famous for its stone collections and the Dawki River. It serves as a visual border between the flat plains of Bangladesh and the sheer cliffs of Meghalaya, India. Stand right at the international border Zero Point and view the famous Dawki suspension bridge on the Indian side. Visit Khasia tribal villages across the river to see their betel leaf gardens and unique stilt houses. The beautifully manicured plain-land tea estates surrounding Jaflong are also worth exploring.

name: Nijhum Dwip
shortDesc: Silent Island — a remote mangrove sanctuary teeming with spotted deer and winter migratory birds.
overview: Translating to 'Silent Island,' this serene location in the Bay of Bengal emerged from the sea in the 1950s. It was later forested by the government and is now essentially a massive mangrove and wildlife sanctuary. The island is renowned for its large population of Chital spotted deer — take a silent boat ride deep into the mangrove canals at dawn to see herds coming to drink. During winter months, the island's mudflats become a sanctuary for thousands of migratory birds. The remote and quiet nature makes it a fantastic off-the-grid camping destination.
activities: Sunrise over the Bay of Bengal
